food_subgroup: Mixed meat and seafood rice bowl (donburi)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for unagi-beef donburi. FOODON_00002809 (rice bowl food product) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_description: Freshwater fish, cooked — used as closest parent for grilled eel component; combined dish has no dedicated FoodEx2 code
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No dedicated FoodEx2 code exists for unagi-beef donburi as a combined dish. A0FJP (freshwater fish, cooked) is used as the closest defensible anchor for the eel component. The beef and rice components are not separately coded here.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for unagi bowl with beef as a combined dish.

main_ingredients: tare sauce (soy sauce, mirin, sake, sugar)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 450 g, comprising a bowl of steamed Japanese rice topped with grilled eel and simmered beef slices glazed with tare sauce, as typically served at Sukiya-style Japanese restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Eel, mixed species, cooked, dry heat (FDC ID 175136) and Beef, short loin, top loin steak, separable lean only, trimmed to 0 inch fat, all grades, cooked, broiled (FDC ID 174032); Japanese short-grain rice, cooked (FDC ID 169756); tare sauce estimated from soy sauce and mirin components
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for unagi bowl with beef as a combined dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component assembly: approximately 80 g grilled eel, 80 g cooked beef, 250 g cooked Japanese rice, and 40 g tare sauce per 450 g serving, then normalised to per-100 g. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries used for each component. Values are cross-checked against typical Japanese donburi nutritional profiles reported in peer-reviewed literature.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice, Japanese short-grain, cooked (GI ~54–56); combined dish GI estimated from rice-dominant carbohydrate fraction
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for unagi bowl with beef as a combined dish. GI is estimated at 54 based on the dominant carbohydrate source being Japanese short-grain white rice (GI 54–56 per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021, Diabetes Care). The protein and fat content from eel and beef will modestly attenuate postprandial glucose response. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (54 × 92.25) / 100 ≈ 50 g (carbohydrate per 450 g serving = 20.5 × 4.5 = 92.25 g).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from tare sauce (soy sauce base)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sauce preparation, as a single 450 g serving provides approximately 1,395 mg sodium — over 60% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2,000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index driven by white rice; moderate glycemic load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that a standard serving delivers approximately 92 g of carbohydrate with a glycemic load of ~50, which will produce a significant postprandial glucose rise; portion reduction or substitution with lower-GI rice is advisable.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium load from tare sauce and moderate saturated fat from beef
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While grilled eel provides cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA), the high sodium content and moderate saturated fat from beef make this dish a moderate cardiovascular concern; individuals with CVD should limit frequency and portion size.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Unagi Bowl with Beef (うなぎ牛丼, Unagi Gyudon) is a Japanese donburi dish combining two iconic Japanese proteins — grilled freshwater eel (unagi) and thinly sliced simmered beef — served over a bowl of steamed Japanese short-grain white rice. The eel is prepared in the traditional kabayaki style: butterflied, skewered, steamed to remove excess fat, then grilled over charcoal or a gas flame and repeatedly basted with a sweet-savoury tare glaze made from soy sauce, mirin, sake, and sugar. The beef component follows the gyudon tradition, where thin slices of beef are simmered in a dashi-based broth seasoned with soy sauce, mirin, and onion until tender and flavourful. The combination of the smoky, caramelised eel and the umami-rich beef creates a layered flavour profile — sweet, savoury, and slightly smoky — over the soft, slightly sticky rice. This dish is a signature offering at Sukiya, a major Japanese fast-casual chain operating in Singapore. Nutritionally, a standard 450 g serving provides approximately 666 kcal, 37 g protein, 17 g fat, and 92 g carbohydrate. The eel contributes meaningful amounts of vitamin D, vitamin B12, vitamin E, and omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA), making it nutritionally distinctive among fast-casual rice bowls. The primary clinical concern is the high sodium content (~1,395 mg per serving) from the tare sauce, which is relevant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The low glycemic index (~54) and high glycemic load (~50 per serving) are relevant for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with fish or soy allergies.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for unagi bowl with beef as a combined dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component assembly using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for grilled eel, cooked beef, cooked Japanese rice, and tare sauce components. GI is estimated from the dominant carbohydrate source (Japanese short-grain white rice, GI 54) per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level USDA data is high quality and the assembly methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-estimated: approximately 80 g grilled eel (USDA FDC 175136), 80 g cooked lean beef (USDA FDC 174032), 250 g cooked Japanese short-grain rice (USDA FDC 169756), and 40 g tare sauce (estimated from soy sauce and mirin) per 450 g serving, normalised to per-100 g. 2. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0; this is confirmed zero for the eel and rice components and trace/negligible for the lean beef component at this portion size — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central component data. 3. GI estimated at 54 from Japanese short-grain white rice as the dominant carbohydrate source; no direct GI measurement exists for this combined dish. 4. Sodium is high due to tare sauce (soy sauce base); exact sodium will vary by restaurant preparation and sauce quantity. 5. The dish is served at Sukiya (Funan) and Funan Mix & Match in Singapore at SGD 17.40. 6. Ontology: no dedicated FoodOn or FoodEx2 code exists for unagi-beef donburi as a combined dish; closest-parent codes used with gap notes. 7. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA component data and are not directly measured for this combined dish.
